message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Migration cinch \"read grants from IAM\" re-invokes the retired lossy-derivation it sits beside","location":"paddock §Cinched decisions migration (lines 132-133) vs §Retired cinches (lines 139-143); memo lines 76-79 (no migration section)","type":"clarity","severity":"medium","issue":"The migration cinch says \"read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster.\" The Retired-cinches block on the same page retires \"Roster derives from IAM\" precisely because \"deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,\" and the memo states a director set is \"several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous matches become representable\" (lines 76-78). Migration actually works only because legacy SAs still carry a role-bearing NAME prefix (director-…/retriever-…, confirmed in RBSDR roster spec regex), so the role — and thus the capability-set — is recoverable from the name, not by lossy reverse-mapping of bindings. The paddock never marks this distinction, so a mount-time reader can read \"read grants from IAM\" as licensing exactly the lossy derivation the adjacent retired cinch forbids. Compounding it: the memo (the mechanism doc the paddock points to) has NO migration section at all.","evidence":"Paddock line 132: \"read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster\"; line 139-141: \"'Roster derives from IAM ...' Retired: under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology\"; memo line 76-78: \"deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping ... partial and ambiguous matches become representable\"","suggested_fix":"Reword the migration cinch to \"derive each legacy citizen's role (and thus its capability-set) from the role-prefixed SA NAME — a one-time bootstrap that works precisely because legacy names still carry the prefix the standing audit cannot rely on — then register the citizen in the declared roster.\" Add a short migration paragraph to the memo (currently absent) covering this plus how grandfathered SAs interact with the orphan marker (next finding).","confidence":"medium"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the actual artifacts. Let me start with the memo and the cult-verb specs cited.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_director_roster}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation rosters
5	{rbtr_director}
6	{giam_service_account_s}
7	in the
8	{rbtgi_depot_project}
9	via per-SA fact-file emission.
10	Read-only observation: lists all SAs, filters to director-prefixed emails, and emits one fact per discovered SA via
11	{buf_write_fact_multi}
12	against the `RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director` extension constant.
13	Fact value is the full SA email; `file_root` is the bare identity extracted from the email.
14	
15	//axhos_step
16	. *Authenticate as
17	{rbtr_governor}*:
18	.. Execute
19	{rbtoe_governor_authenticate}
20	.. {rbbc_store}
21	`«GOVERNOR_TOKEN»` from the resulting access token
22	
23	//axhos_step
24	. *List service accounts*:
25	.. {rbbc_call}
26	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/list[iam.projects.serviceAccounts.list] with:
27	... method: `GET`
28	... endpoint: `https://iam.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{rbtgi_depot_project}/serviceAccounts`
29	... header: `Authorization: Bearer «GOVERNOR_TOKEN»`
30	.. {rbbc_require}
31	HTTP 200 response
32	.. {rbbc_store}
33	`«ACCOUNTS»` from response `.accounts[]` array
34	
35	//axhos_step
36	. *Filter to director service accounts*:
37	.. From `«ACCOUNTS»`, read the `.email` field of each entry and test whether it matches `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}$`
38	.. {rbbc_store}
39	`«DIRECTOR_EMAILS»` — the matching emails; per-role observation per BBAAQ contract
40	
41	//axhos_step
42	. *Emit Fact Per Discovered {rbtr_director}*:
43	.. For each `«SA_EMAIL»` in `«DIRECTOR_EMAILS»`:
44	... {rbbc_store}
45	`«IDENTITY»` from the regex capture group — the portion of `«SA_EMAIL»` between `${RBCC_account_director}-` and `@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}`
46	... {rbbc_call}
47	{buf_write_fact_multi}
48	with three positional arguments:
49	.... `<file_root>`: `«IDENTITY»` (bare identity — the basename root for the emitted fact file)
50	.... `<ext>`: `RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director` (resolves at source time to `roster-director`)
51	.... `<value>`: `«SA_EMAIL»` (the full service account email address)
52	
53	//axhos_step
54	. *Report count*:
55	.. {rbbc_show}
56	total count of emitted director fact files
57	
58	//axhoc_completion
59	Successful execution rosters {rbtr_director} service accounts via per-SA fact emission — one `roster-director` fact file per discovered identity, each containing the full SA email as its value.
60	The operation is read-only; no service accounts are created or modified.
61

content.text:
Now I have key evidence. Let me note what I've confirmed:

1. The memo (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md) has **NO migration section**. Confirmed — I read all 241 lines and there is no migration heading or migration cinch anywhere in the memo. The memo even discusses "the role prefix leaves the identity name" (line 73) as the new state, and discusses one-time migrations only for the *governor name* (lines 209-210) and the per-person migration (line 236).

2. The RBSDR roster spec confirms the **role-prefixed SA NAME** mechanism: line 37 matches `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@...`, and line 45 extracts the identity "between `${RBCC_account_director}-` and `@`". So legacy SAs DO carry a role-bearing name prefix, and the role IS recoverable from the name.

The finding's central factual claims about the memo and the roster spec are substantiated. However, the finding's location references depend on the PADDOCK — and the paddock text was given to me as `undefined`. Let me check whether I can recover the actual paddock content, since the finding makes specific claims about paddock lines 132-143.

input.description: Locate paddock and gallops state files for heat BZ

content.text: The CLAUDE.md instructions are explicit: never read the gallops JSON or harness-persisted gazette files directly. But there is a paddock file at `jjp_uBuZ.md`. The paddock content was given to me as `undefined` in the prompt, which means the orchestrator could not supply it. The proper interface is `jjx_brief`/`jjx_paddock`, but the paddock file `jjp_uBuZ.md` is the actual committed paddock document (not a harness-persisted tool-result blob or the gallops JSON). Let me read it directly since it is the source artifact under review.

content.content:
1	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
2	
3	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
4	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
5	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
6	Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, and the
7	RBRD mode-enum activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation";
8	memo-20260527).
9	
10	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
11	the declared roster, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
12	in it — it swaps the identity-kind (citizen → federate) and the token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and
13	nothing else. That is what makes the switch non-disruptive.
14	
15	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
16	paddock holds shape only.
17	
18	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
19	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
20	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
21	identity-keyed one. Update that memo when the model is frozen.
22	
23	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
24	
25	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), a depot-issued SA
26	  holding one RBRA key. Rank-neutral: rank is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the
27	  capability-sets the citizen holds. Multi-role is first-class — one citizen holds several
28	  capability-sets. (The federation tier's identity is a *federate*; the payor is the founder,
29	  outside both.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities; this is native to the GCP
32	  primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings). Today's `invest` fuses the two; the model
33	  unfuses them.
34	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
35	  bodies). Tier-blind.
36	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-roster,
37	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
38	  distinct concepts.
39	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
40	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
41	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
42	  guard that keeps the roster auditable.
43	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
44	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
45	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it.
46	
47	## Intent vs enforcement — the declared roster (cinched)
48	
49	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
50	is stored in a depot-resident **declared roster**, distinct from IAM. The audit diffs declared
51	intent against IAM; routine roster reads intent and is identical across tiers. Federation forces
52	the same roster anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Why it must be stored, and where it lives:
53	memo.)
54	
55	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
56	
57	The audit measures drift between declared intent and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the asymmetry
58	is the Pale recognition test:
59	
60	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
61	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
62	  adjudicates. No verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
63	
64	Auto-converge presumes roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (else it
65	is a softer escalation path) — a security constraint on where the roster lives; see Open. (Drift
66	taxonomy, the intent-first bypass signal, definition-change versioning, and the orphan marker:
67	memo.)
68	
69	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
70	
71	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
72	
73	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
74	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
75	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
76	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
77	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
78	  governor buys nothing.
79	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
80	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
81	  are parameters).
82	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
83	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
84	
85	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
86	
87	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
88	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
89	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
90	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
91	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
92	
93	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
94	
95	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
96	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
97	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism, and the standalone fallback if the governor is descoped:
98	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
99	
100	## What done looks like
101	
102	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
103	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/
104	mantle → add/grant/revoke/remove + read, parameterized by capability-set), cloud-side bash wherever
105	it names roles, the .adoc specs that voice the cult verbs, the Rust theurge cases that drive them,
106	and the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
107	throughout.
108	
109	The load-bearing federation-enabling core within that surface is narrow: route the
110	role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing
111	memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The vocabulary convergence is the larger,
112	motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is deferred — recorded here as shape.
113	
114	## Cinched decisions
115	
116	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
117	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (the federation tier's peer is a
118	  *federate*; the payor is the founder, outside both). Identity lifecycle is decoupled from
119	  capability lifecycle. Multi-role is first-class.
120	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
121	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
122	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
123	- The declared roster (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
124	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; no verb auto-revokes —
125	  with auto-converge presuming roster-write is at least as protected as grant.
126	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
127	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
128	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
129	  not in this heat.
130	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the
131	  declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.
132	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
133	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
134	
135	## Retired cinches
136	
137	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
138	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
139	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared roster +
140	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
141	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
142	
143	## Open — resolve within the heat
144	
145	- Where the declared roster physically lives (GCS object in the depot bucket vs GAR artifact) and
146	  its writer/etag discipline — a security boundary, not just storage: roster-write authority must be
147	  at least as protected as the grant verbs (the auto-heal invariant).
148	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
149	  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
150	- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel.
151	- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
152	  two-gesture add-then-grant flow itself follows from the decoupling).
153	
154	## Sources
155	
156	The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this heat;
157	memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle split — the enabler);
158	memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb shape — to be updated per
159	the divergence above); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak (standalone governor-leak fix);
160	memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock points to); RBSHR "Operator
161	federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and the 260605 review pass.

content.text:
Now I have the actual paddock. Let me check the finding's specific claims against it.

The finding says:
- "paddock §Cinched decisions migration (lines 132-133)" — actually the migration cinch is at **lines 130-131**, not 132-133. Content: "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day."
- "§Retired cinches (lines 139-143)" — the retired cinch is at lines 137-141.

The line numbers in the finding are off, but the durable content claims are checkable. Let me now examine the core argument carefully.

**Finding's central claim:** The migration cinch says "read grants from IAM" — this re-invokes the lossy IAM-derivation that the adjacent retired cinch forbids. The finding argues migration actually works because legacy SAs carry a role-bearing NAME prefix (recoverable role), NOT by lossy reverse-mapping of bindings.

Let me reason about this carefully against the actual text:

The migration cinch (line 130-131) reads: "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out."

Critically, the cinch ITSELF references "role-named SAs" and "legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names." So the paddock already grounds the migration in the role-named SA mechanism. The phrase "read grants from IAM" is describing reading the *actual current bindings* to know what to register — which is a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the *standing* roster-derives-from-IAM that the retired cinch forbids.

Let me check the retired cinch precisely: "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired because "deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology."

The key distinction: the retired cinch was about a **standing/continuous** derivation (the roster IS IAM, no stored list). The migration cinch is a **one-time bootstrap** that *populates* the stored roster (which then stands independently). These are genuinely different operations.

Let me verify whether the finding's claim that "migration works precisely because of the name prefix, not binding-derivation" is correct, and whether the paddock actually conflates them.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_director_invest}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation invests a
5	{rbtr_director}
6	{giam_service_account}
7	into the
8	{rbtgi_depot_project}.
9	The
10	{rbtr_governor}
11	mints the SA, generates its credential key, and writes the RBRA bytes to the assay output slot.
12	The operation is identity-parameterized: a required identity folio (param1) is supplied by the operator; the SA account name is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`.
13	Idempotent establish: a pre-existing SA is not drift — its identity (email, `uniqueId`) and IAM bindings are preserved and only the credential key is rotated; an absent SA is created first. Either path ends with exactly one fresh USER_MANAGED key and the role's bindings in place.
14	
15	//axhos_step
16	. *Authenticate as
17	{rbtr_governor}*:
18	.. Execute
19	{rbtoe_governor_authenticate}
20	.. {rbbc_store}
21	`«GOVERNOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
22	
23	//axhos_step
24	. *Compose
25	{rbtr_director}
26	Service Account Identity*:
27	.. {rbbc_require}
28	identity folio (param1) is non-empty
29	.. {rbbc_store}
30	`«DIRECTOR_ACCOUNT_NAME»` as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>` (operator-supplied identity composing the account name)
31	.. {rbbc_store}
32	`«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` as `«DIRECTOR_ACCOUNT_NAME»@${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}.iam.gserviceaccount.com` (depot project ID derived from `(RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX, RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER)` at regime kindle time)
33	
34	//axhos_step
35	. *Preflight: Detect Existing Service Account*:
36	.. {rbbc_call}
37	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/get[iam.serviceAccounts.get] for `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»`
38	.. {rbbc_store}
39	`«SA_EXISTS»` true on HTTP 200 (the SA and its bindings are kept; only the key is rotated below), false on HTTP 404 (the SA is created below)
40	.. {rbbc_fatal}
41	on any other HTTP code — an indeterminate preflight is not a re-key signal
42	
43	//axhos_step
44	. *Create
45	{rbtr_director}
46	Service Account* (when `«SA_EXISTS»` is false):
47	.. {rbbc_call}
48	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[iam.serviceAccounts.create] with:
49	... accountId: `«DIRECTOR_ACCOUNT_NAME»`
50	... displayName: `Recipe Bottle Director (<identity>)`
51	... description: `Create/destroy container images for <identity>`
52	... project:
53	{rbtgi_depot_project}
54	.. {rbbc_require}
55	HTTP 200 response
56	.. {rbbc_store}
57	`«DIRECTOR_UNIQUE_ID»` from response `.uniqueId` field
58	.. NOTE: Skipped when `«SA_EXISTS»` is true — the identity is preserved and `«DIRECTOR_UNIQUE_ID»` is read from the preflight response instead.
59	
60	//axhos_step
61	. *Verify Service Account Propagation* (when `«SA_EXISTS»` is false):
62	.. {rbbc_call}
63	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/get[iam.serviceAccounts.get] for `projects/-/serviceAccounts/«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»`
64	.. {rbbc_poll}
65	with backoff until HTTP 200 (newly-created SA may not be immediately resolvable by email)
66	.. NOTE: Poll must use the email address, not the numeric ID. The email-based lookup path has independent propagation from the UID-based path, and subsequent steps (keys.list, keys.create) all use the email address. Polling by UID can succeed while the email path still returns 404.
67	.. NOTE: Skipped when `«SA_EXISTS»` is true — a pre-existing SA is already propagated.
68	.. {rbbc_require}
69	service account accessible by email
70	
71	//axhos_step
72	. *Blunt-Kill Existing Keys*:
73	.. {rbbc_call}
74	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list[serviceAccounts.keys.list] for `«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»`
75	.. Retry up to `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX` times on HTTP 404 with `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC` delay between attempts
76	.. NOTE: Keys subresource read-path may lag SA email-path readiness — the SA GET probe (previous step) can return 200 while `keys.list` on the same email immediately returns 404, observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap on freshly-minted depots. The 404 confirms `keys.list` returned no state, making retry safe. Shares budget with step 7 keys.create — same underlying physics (subresource visibility after parent creation). See `Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` for transcript evidence and <<rbscip_trade_study>> for the doctrinal pattern.
77	.. {rbbc_store}
78	`«EXISTING_KEYS»` as the `.name` of every `.keys[]` entry with `keyType: USER_MANAGED`
79	.. {rbbc_call}
80	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/delete[serviceAccounts.keys.delete] for each `«EXISTING_KEYS»` entry
81	.. {rbbc_require}
82	HTTP 200 OR HTTP 404 (key already gone — idempotent) for each delete
83	.. NOTE: Re-key rotates the Key lifecycle only — the SA and its IAM bindings are untouched. Deleting every user-managed key before minting guarantees exactly one fresh key when this operation completes (the blunt kill). This replaces the former fail-loud "manual cleanup via Console" abort.
84	
85	//axhos_step
86	. *Generate Service Account Key*:
87	.. {rbbc_call}
88	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/create[serviceAccounts.keys.create] for `«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` with:
89	... privateKeyType: `TYPE_GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS_FILE`
90	.. Retry up to `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX` times on HTTP 404 with `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC` delay between attempts
91	.. NOTE: SA read operations (GET by email, keys.list) may succeed while keys.create POST returns 404 "does not exist" due to write-path propagation delay. Google link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[documents]: "you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account." The 404 confirms no key was created, making retry safe. See <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>.
92	.. {rbbc_store}
93	`«DIRECTOR_PRIVATE_KEY_DATA»` from response `.privateKeyData` field
94	.. {rbtoe_rbra_generate}
95	converts `«DIRECTOR_PRIVATE_KEY_DATA»` to `«DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE»`
96	
97	//axhos_step
98	. *Get Depot Project Number*:
99	.. {rbbc_call}
100	link:https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/reference/rest/v3/projects/get[projects.get] for
101	{rbtgi_depot_project}
102	.. {rbbc_store}
103	`«PROJECT_NUMBER»` from `.projectNumber` field
104	
105	//axhos_step
106	. *Grant Project-Level Permissions*:
107	.. {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} for

109	.. Add `serviceAccount:«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` with roles:
110	... `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` (submit and manage builds)
111	... `roles/viewer` (read project resources)
112	... `roles/cloudbuild.workerPoolUser` (use private pool for vouch `builds.create`)
113	.. Policy version: 3
114	.. {rbbc_require}
115	IAM policy update successful
116	
117	//axhos_step
118	. *Grant
119	{rbtr_mason}
120	Impersonation*:
121	.. NOTE:
122	{rbtr_director}
123	submits builds that execute as
124	{rbtr_mason}.
125	This requires `serviceAccountUser` permission on
126	{rbtr_mason}.
127	.. {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa} on
128	{rbtr_mason}
129	service account

131	.. {rbbc_require}
132	IAM binding successful
133	
134	//axhos_step
135	. *Grant
136	{rbtgi_build_bucket}
137	Access*:
138	.. {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket} on
139	{rbtgi_build_bucket}
140	.. Add `serviceAccount:«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` with roles:
141	... `roles/storage.objectCreator` (upload sources)
142	... `roles/storage.objectViewer` (verify uploads)
143	.. {rbbc_require}
144	bucket IAM update successful
145	
146	//axhos_step
147	. *Grant
148	{rbtgi_repository}
149	Admin Access*:
150	.. {rbbc_call}
151	link:https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories/getIamPolicy[repositories.getIamPolicy] via HTTP GET with `options.requestedPolicyVersion=3` query parameter
152	.. NOTE:
153	{gar_registry}
154	proto specifies GET for `getIamPolicy` with no `additional_bindings` for POST. Using POST fails intermittently with HTTP 404 (transcoding layer rejection, not missing repository). See <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>.
155	.. NOTE: Resource-scope IAM cache lag — on a freshly-levied depot, the governor's `roles/owner` grant may not yet have reached the AR-resource-scope IAM cache, even though it has reached project-scope and SA-scope caches.  This surfaces as HTTP 403 on the very first `getIamPolicy` call.  The grant harness absorbs this via class C tolerance (HTTP 403 with empty body-glob, time-bounded against `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC`) — see <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> propagation catalog.  Real propagation succeeds within budget; real denial waits the budget and fails cleanly.

157	HTTP 200 — 404 means repository does not exist (real error, not empty policy)
158	.. {rbbc_call}
159	link:https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories/setIamPolicy[repositories.setIamPolicy] for
160	{rbtgi_repository}
161	.. *Complete policy write*: Add ALL expected bindings to the read policy, regardless of what the read returned:
162	... `serviceAccount:«DIRECTOR_EMAIL»` with `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` — Director manages images
163	... `serviceAccount:«MASON_EMAIL»` with `roles/artifactregistry.writer` — Mason pushes images at build time
164	.. NOTE: Same complete-policy-write pattern as the secret IAM step. Prevents stale read from overwriting Mason's writer binding set by
165	{rbtgo_depot_levy}.
166	.. {rbbc_require}
167	repository IAM set with all expected bindings
168

171	. *Deliver Credential to Assay Output Slot*:
172	.. {rbbc_store}

content.text:
This confirms the director invest spec: it grants bindings across **project** (step "Grant Project-Level Permissions"), **Mason SA** (step "Grant Mason Impersonation"), **build bucket** (step "Grant build_bucket Access"), and **repository** (step "Grant repository Admin Access"). So the memo's claim (lines 75-77) that a director set is "several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket" is accurate.

This also confirms the finding's claim that the role/capability-set IS recoverable from the SA **name** prefix (`${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`), and that the bindings themselves are scattered. So if you wanted to reconstruct a director's full capability set purely from IAM bindings (without the name), you'd face the lossy reverse-mapping problem the memo describes.

Now let me evaluate the finding's core argument with precision.

**The finding's claim:** The migration cinch's "read grants from IAM" re-invokes the lossy derivation the adjacent retired cinch forbids; migration actually works via the name prefix, not binding-derivation; the paddock never marks this distinction.

Let me re-read the migration cinch (paddock line 130-131) word for word:

> "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day."

Analyzing this critically:

1. The cinch **already names** "role-named SAs" and "legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names." So the paddock DOES ground migration in the role-named mechanism — the very distinction the finding says is "never marked."

2. The phrase "read grants from IAM" — what does it actually mean here? There are two readings:
   - (a) The finding's reading: derive *which capability-set* each SA holds by reverse-mapping its bindings (lossy).
   - (b) Alternative reading: the role is already known from the name prefix (`director-`/`retriever-`); "read grants from IAM" means read the *actual current bindings* to confirm/populate, and "register citizens in the declared roster" records the now-known {identity → capability-set}.

The finding asserts reading (a) is what a "mount-time reader can read." But the cinch's own framing — "grandfathers existing **role-named** SAs ... let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out" — strongly signals that the role comes from the name. The name prefix is the load-bearing element explicitly stated.

So is there a genuine conflation? Let me consider the strongest version of the finding. The retired cinch forbids "Roster derives from IAM ... it is lossy." The migration cinch says "read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster." There IS surface tension: both say "from IAM," and a careless reader could see contradiction. But:

- The retired cinch is about a **standing/continuous** equivalence (roster ≡ IAM, no stored list, "it cannot drift").
- The migration cinch is a **one-time bootstrap** that populates a stored roster which thereafter stands independently and CAN drift (and is audited).

These are genuinely different. The one-time-vs-standing distinction is the real resolution, and the finding's proposed fix actually captures it ("a one-time bootstrap that works precisely because legacy names still carry the prefix the standing audit cannot rely on"). 

Now — is the distinction "never marked" in the paddock, as the finding claims? Let me check. The migration cinch says "grandfathers existing role-named SAs" and "let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out." This explicitly invokes the name-prefix. So the finding's claim that "the paddock never marks this distinction" is **partially overstated** — the paddock DOES reference the role-named SAs in the same sentence. What the paddock does NOT do is (a) explicitly say the role/capability-set is recovered *from the name* (it says "read grants from IAM"), and (b) explicitly mark this as a *one-time bootstrap distinct from the standing-derivation* the retired cinch forbids.

Let me also verify the finding's claim that the memo has NO migration section. I read the full memo (241 lines) — confirmed, there is no migration section. The only migration mentions are (line 210) "a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors" (governor-name migration) and (line 236) "the per-person migration." Neither covers the legacy role-named-SA grandfathering. So the paddock's migration cinch points to "memo" for mechanism generally, but the migration mechanism itself is genuinely absent from the memo. That is a real gap.

Let me now weigh the overall verdict. Let me also confirm the credential-format spec to be sure the name prefix is the canonical role-bearing element and there's no SA label/tag carrying role today.

input.command: grep -n "label\|tag\|description\|rbk-identity\|prefix\|RBCC_account" /home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc | head -40; echo "=== RBCC_account defs ==="; grep -rn "RBCC_account_director\|RBCC_account_retriever\|RBCC_account_governor" /home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/*.sh 2>/dev/null | head
input.description: Check credential format spec for role-bearing markers and account prefix constants

content.text:
This confirms the role is carried by the SA **name** prefix (`${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`), and there is no role-bearing SA label or tag today (consistent with the retired cinch noting "SA labels do not exist"). The role IS recoverable from the legacy name; the bindings alone are not a clean source.

I now have enough to render a verdict.

**Assessment:**

The finding has a genuine kernel but overstates its case in a key way:

1. **Real:** The migration cinch phrasing "read grants from IAM" is in surface tension with the adjacent retired cinch that forbids deriving the roster from IAM as "lossy." A careless mount-time reader could read these as contradictory.

2. **Real:** The role/capability-set is recoverable from the legacy SA *name* prefix (`director-`/`retriever-`), not from clean binding reverse-mapping — confirmed by RBSDR (regex on name), RBSDK (scattered bindings), and the `RBCC_account_*` constants.

3. **Real:** The memo has NO migration section at all, despite the paddock cinch pointing migration mechanism to "memo."

4. **Overstated:** The finding claims "the paddock never marks this distinction." But the migration cinch ITSELF says "grandfathers existing **role-named** SAs" and "let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out" — the name-prefix mechanism IS named in the same sentence. So the distinction is partly marked. What is missing is the explicit one-time-bootstrap-vs-standing-derivation framing and the explicit statement that the role comes *from the name*, not from reverse-mapping bindings.

5. **Minor:** The cited line numbers are off (migration is at 130-131, retired at 137-141; finding says 132-133 and 139-143). This is a stale-by-mount artifact, not load-bearing.

The finding is **real but partial** — the issue is genuine (a clarity hazard worth fixing), but the "never marks this distinction" framing is imprecise, and the proposed fix's parenthetical is slightly more contradictory than warranted. Severity is genuinely **low-to-medium**: it is a clarity polish on an already-grounded cinch (the cinch already names the role-named SAs), not a substantive design gap. The memo-has-no-migration-section observation is the more valuable half.

Let me refine the fix to be precise and load-bearing.

input.reasoning:
I independently verified each evidentiary claim against the source artifacts.

MEMO (Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md, read in full, 241 lines): Confirmed it has NO migration section. The only migration references are line 210 ("a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors" — governor-name migration) and line 236 ("the per-person migration"). Neither covers grandfathering legacy role-named operator SAs. Lines 75-78 confirm the memo's lossy-reverse-mapping argument: a director set is "several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous matches become representable." So the finding's memo quotes are accurate, and the no-migration-section claim is TRUE.

PADDOCK (.claude/jjm/jjp_uBuZ.md — the actual committed paddock, which the prompt supplied as "undefined"): The migration cinch is at lines 130-131 (finding said 132-133 — stale by ~2 lines), the retired cinch at lines 137-141 (finding said 139-143). Line-number drift is the only inaccuracy in the citations; durable content matches.

The migration cinch reads: "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day." The retired cinch retires "Roster derives from IAM ... lossy ... tautology."

CULT-VERB SPECS: RBSDR roster spec confirms role is recovered from the SA NAME prefix via regex `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@...` (lines 37, 45). RBSDK invest spec confirms director bindings are scattered across project/Mason-SA/bucket/repo (steps "Grant Project-Level / Mason Impersonation / build_bucket / repository Admin"). RBCC_account_* constants in rbgg_Governor.sh confirm name-prefix composition (`${RBCC_account_director}-${identity}`). So the finding's claim — migration works via the recoverable name prefix, not clean binding reverse-mapping — is technically correct.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERSTATES: It claims "the paddock never marks this distinction," but the migration cinch ITSELF says "grandfathers existing role-named SAs" and "let legacy director-…/retriever-… names age out" — the name-prefix mechanism is named in the very same sentence. So the distinction is partially marked already; what is missing is (a) the explicit statement that the role is recovered FROM THE NAME (the cinch's "read grants from IAM" muddies this) and (b) the one-time-bootstrap vs standing-derivation framing that cleanly resolves the surface tension with the retired cinch. The genuine, narrow defect is the phrase "read grants from IAM" sitting beside a retired cinch that forbids IAM-derivation, with no inline marker that this is a one-time populate-the-stored-roster step rather than the forbidden standing equivalence.

Net: the issue is real (a clarity hazard + a genuinely absent memo migration section), but the finding's framing ("never marks this distinction") is imprecise and its self-evidence partly overstated. Hence partial. Severity is low-to-medium — the cinch already grounds itself in role-named SAs, so this is polish on a mostly-correct cinch plus a real doc gap, not a design hole.
input.refined_fix:
Two edits, both small:

1. Paddock migration cinch (jjp_uBuZ.md lines 130-131): replace the ambiguous "read grants from IAM" with language that names the recovery source and marks it as a one-time bootstrap, distinguishing it from the retired standing-derivation. E.g.: "Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each legacy citizen's role — and thus its capability-set — from the role-prefixed SA name (director-…/retriever-…), then register that citizen+set in the declared roster as a one-time bootstrap. This is not the retired 'roster derives from IAM' equivalence — it populates a stored, drift-able roster once, using the name prefix the standing audit cannot rely on; thereafter the roster stands independently. Legacy names age out; no flag day." Do NOT adopt the candidate fix's parenthetical verbatim ("works precisely because legacy names still carry the prefix the standing audit cannot rely on") unedited — it is correct but compressed; the expanded form above is clearer.

2. Memo (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md): add a short Migration section (currently absent — verified). Cover: role recovered from the legacy SA name prefix (cite the RBSDR roster regex and the RBCC_account_* composition as the existing mechanism); the bootstrap reads current bindings only to confirm/seed, not to derive set membership (avoiding the lossy reverse-mapping the roster section already rejects); and how grandfathered SAs interact with the orphan marker (legacy role-named SAs predate the marker, so the orphan sweep must treat name-prefixed SAs as known-managed during the age-out window). Drop the candidate's line-number citations (paddock migration is 130-131, retired 137-141, not 132-133/139-143) — they are stale.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial verification and submitted my structured verdict.

Summary: The finding is **partial** at **low** severity. Its factual claims check out — the memo at `/home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` genuinely has no migration section, and the role is recoverable from the legacy SA name prefix (confirmed via RBSDR's `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@` regex, RBSDK's scattered bindings, and the `RBCC_account_*` constants in `rbgg_Governor.sh`). But the finding overstates by claiming "the paddock never marks this distinction" — the migration cinch (actually at lines 130-131 of `.claude/jjm/jjp_uBuZ.md`, not 132-133) already says "grandfathers existing **role-named** SAs ... let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out," so the name-prefix mechanism is named in the same sentence. The genuine defect is narrower: the phrase "read grants from IAM" sits beside the retired lossy-IAM-derivation cinch with no inline marker that this is a one-time stored-roster bootstrap rather than the forbidden standing equivalence, plus the real doc gap of no migration section in the memo it points to.
